name: Far Darrig
description: The Far Darrig, also known as Fear Dearg, is a solitary malevolent fairy from Irish folklore, distinct from the more commonly known leprechaun or clurichaun. Often considered one of the most dangerous of the solitary fairies, the Far Darrig is known for his cruel jests and malicious pranks. Unlike other fairies who might be appeased or avoided, the Far Darrig actively seeks out human company for the sole purpose of causing mischief and harm. He is a harbinger of nightmares and ill fortune, often associated with causing sickness and physical ailments. The Far Darrig is believed to roam at night, delighting in tormenting sleepers and spreading general unease. He is a figure of fear and caution in Irish folklore, representing the darker aspects of the fairy realm.
subtitle: A malevolent solitary fairy of Irish folklore.
appearance: The Far Darrig is typically described as a small, grotesque figure, often depicted as having a misshapen face and wild, unruly hair. He is often seen wearing a red coat or jacket, which distinguishes him from the green attire of the leprechaun. Some accounts describe him as having a long tail or other animalistic features, emphasizing his connection to the wild and untamed aspects of the natural world.
habitat: Solitary places, forests, remote areas, and near human settlements at night.
behavior: The Far Darrig is known for his cruel and malicious behavior. He delights in playing pranks, spreading misfortune, and causing nightmares. He is also believed to be a bringer of disease and illness. Unlike some other fairies, he is not easily appeased and actively seeks out human company to torment.

keyMyths.title: The Far Darrig and the Sleeper
keyMyths.description: A farmer falls asleep in his field and is tormented by a Far Darrig all night long. The fairy inflicts nightmares and physical discomfort, leaving the farmer exhausted and ill the next day, showcasing the Far Darrig's ability to torment sleepers and cause lasting harm. The farmer only recovers after seeking the help of a local wise woman.
keyMyths.source: Briggs, Katharine Mary. *An Encyclopedia of Fairies*.

keyMyths.title: The Red-Coated Mischief-Maker
keyMyths.description: A story tells of a village plagued by a series of unfortunate events – crops failing, livestock dying, and unexplained accidents. The villagers eventually realize that a Far Darrig is responsible, identifiable by his distinctive red coat. They band together and use iron tools and salt to drive him away, restoring peace to their community.
keyMyths.source: Lysaght, Patricia. *The Banshee: The Irish Death-Messenger*.

keyMyths.title: The Far Darrig's Deceit
keyMyths.description: A traveler encounters a seemingly harmless old man in the woods, who offers him shelter for the night. Unbeknownst to the traveler, the old man is a Far Darrig in disguise. Throughout the night, the traveler is subjected to increasingly disturbing illusions and pranks, barely escaping with his sanity intact, highlighting the Far Darrig's deceptive nature.
keyMyths.source: Wilde, Lady. *Ancient Legends, Mystic Charms, and Superstitions of Ireland*.

sources.author: Briggs, Katharine Mary
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to fairies and other supernatural creatures from folklore.
sources.text: A comprehensive guide to fairies and other supernatural creatures from folklore.
